section.title: More Proverbs of Solomon
section.sectionRationale: A new collection begins here, explicitly labeled 'additional proverbs of Solomon, copied by the men of Hezekiah king of Judah,' 25:1, a scribal school project centuries after Solomon that gathered and preserved royal wisdom for a new generation. This chapter opens with kings and courts and moves through vivid, image-rich proverbs on timing, self-control, and honest speech.
section.thesis: It is the glory of kings to search out what God conceals, and true wisdom is patient, restrained, and fitly spoken, since a word rightly timed is like apples of gold in silver settings, while pride, gossip, and a temper out of control leave a life like a city with broken walls.
section.frameNote: Five clusters: the glory of God and kings, and self-restraint before rulers; the fitly spoken word and the trustworthy messenger; kindness to an enemy and the danger of overexposure; honey in moderation and the danger of a quarrelsome tongue; self-control as the true wall of a life.

units.label: The glory of God and kings
units.note: concealing and searching out a matter

verses.text: These are additional proverbs of Solomon, which were copied by the men of Hezekiah king of Judah:
It is the glory of God to conceal a matter and the glory of kings to search it out.
As the heavens are high and the earth is deep, so the hearts of kings cannot be searched.
Remove the dross from the silver, and a vessel for a silversmith will come forth.
Remove the wicked from the king's presence, and his throne will be established in righteousness.
Do not exalt yourself in the presence of the king, and do not stand in the place of great men;
for it is better that he says to you, "Come up here!" than that you should be demoted in the presence of the prince. Even what you have seen with your own eyes,
do not bring hastily to court. Otherwise, what will you do in the end when your neighbor puts you to shame?

units.label: The fitly spoken word
units.note: apples of gold, the trustworthy messenger

verses.text: Argue your case with your neighbor without betraying another's confidence,
lest the one who hears may disgrace you, and your infamy never go away.
A word {{davar|fitly}} spoken is like apples of gold in settings of silver.
Like an earring of gold or an ornament of fine gold is a wise man's rebuke to a listening ear.
Like the cold of snow in the time of harvest is a trustworthy messenger to those who send him; he refreshes the soul of his masters.
Like clouds and wind without rain is the man who boasts of gifts never given.

units.label: Patience, kindness to an enemy
units.note: coals of fire, burning coals of kindness

verses.text: Through patience a ruler can be persuaded, and a gentle tongue can break a bone.
If you find honey, eat just what you need, lest you have too much and vomit it up.
Seldom set foot in your neighbor's house, lest he grow weary and hate you.
Like a club or sword or sharp arrow is a man who bears false witness against his neighbor.
Like a broken tooth or a foot out of joint is confidence in a faithless man in time of trouble.
Like one who removes a garment on a cold day or vinegar poured on a wound is one who sings songs to a heavy heart.
If your enemy is hungry, give him food to eat, and if he is thirsty, give him water to drink.
For in so doing, you will heap burning coals on his head, and the LORD will reward you.

units.label: Honey, gossip, and self-control
units.note: a city whose walls are broken down

verses.text: As the north wind brings forth rain, so a backbiting tongue brings angry looks.
Better to live on a corner of the roof than to share a house with a quarrelsome wife.
Like cold water to a weary soul is good news from a distant land.
Like a muddied spring or a polluted well is a righteous man who gives way to the wicked.
It is not good to eat too much honey or to search out one's own glory.
Like a city whose walls are broken down is a man who does not control his {{ruach|temper}}.

davar.gloss: Fitly spoken, literally 'a word upon its wheels' or in its proper setting. Verse 11 pictures such a word as apples of gold in silver settings, treasuring not just true speech but speech rightly timed and rightly placed, the same concern for timing that runs through the whole chapter.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, breath, temper. Verse 28 pictures a man who cannot control his ruach as a city with broken walls, utterly exposed to attack; the same word elsewhere names the Spirit of God, showing how central self-mastery of one's inner spirit is to the whole biblical picture of wisdom.

basic.context: A new collection opens here, explicitly credited to Hezekiah's scribes who preserved older Solomonic proverbs generations later. The chapter moves from kings and courts, restraint before rulers, and honest testimony, through a string of vivid similes about well-timed words, patience, kindness to enemies, and finally self-control, pictured as the wall that keeps a life from being overrun.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter teaches largely through simile, 'like apples of gold,' 'like a broken tooth,' 'like a city with broken walls,' inviting the reader to feel the truth in a vivid image rather than merely accepting an abstract rule. As elsewhere in Proverbs, these are wise generalizations about how patience, honesty, and self-control tend to work, not legal guarantees for every situation.
basic.originalLanguages: The Hebrew phrase behind 'fitly spoken,' verse 11, literally means 'a word upon its wheels,' picturing speech that moves smoothly, rightly timed and rightly placed, like apples of gold nestled in silver settings. And ruach, spirit or temper, verse 28, pictures the self as a walled city, so that a person who cannot control this inner spirit stands defenseless, like a city whose walls have fallen.
basic.typology: Kindness to a hungry, thirsty enemy that heaps burning coals of shame and softening on his head, verses 21-22, anticipates Christ's command to love enemies and Paul's direct quotation of this proverb in Romans 12:20. The king's glory in searching out what God conceals, verse 2, foreshadows Christ, the greater Solomon, in whom all the hidden treasures of wisdom and knowledge are found, Colossians 2:3.
basic.christ: Paul directly quotes verses 21-22 in Romans 12:20, commanding believers to feed a hungry enemy and give a thirsty one drink, heaping burning coals on his head, showing that Christ's ethic of enemy love was already present, in seed form, in Solomon's wisdom, and now finds its full shape in the cross, where Christ loved and died for His enemies, Romans 5:10. And the chapter's praise of the king who searches out what is concealed, verse 2, points to Christ, the true king in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, Colossians 2:3.
basic.god: It is God's own glory to conceal a matter, verse 2, leaving mystery even for kings to seek out, showing that not all things are meant to be immediately plain, and that the search for wisdom itself honors Him. He also rewards kindness shown to an enemy, verse 22, revealing a character concerned not merely with justice but with the possibility of an enemy's repentance and change.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 12:20 directly quotes verses 21-22 on kindness to an enemy. Colossians 2:3 echoes the hidden treasures a greater king searches out, verse 2. Matthew 5:44 develops love for enemies commanded here in seed form. James 3:2 echoes verse 28's concern for self-control as the mark of a mature life.
basic.oneStory: This chapter honors kings who search out God's hidden glory, prizes well-timed and honest speech, and commands kindness even toward enemies. All of this reaches its fullest form in Christ, the true king in whom wisdom's treasures are hidden, and whose own enemy love at the cross fulfills what this chapter could only sketch.

context.literary: Chapter 25 opens a distinct collection, 25:1 to 29:27, explicitly credited to scribes working under King Hezekiah, roughly two and a half centuries after Solomon, who gathered and copied older royal proverbs for their own generation's instruction.
context.historical: Hezekiah's reign, around 715 to 686 BC, included a religious and literary revival, 2 Chronicles 29-31, making this scribal preservation project a natural part of restoring Israel's covenant heritage, including its wisdom literature, after the neglect of prior kings.
context.canonical: Verses 21-22's command to feed a hungry enemy is quoted directly by Paul in Romans 12:20, one of the clearest examples in the New Testament of a specific Old Testament proverb being taken up nearly word for word as apostolic ethical teaching.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To instruct through vivid, memorable similes on restraint before rulers, well-timed and honest speech, patient kindness even toward enemies, and self-control as the foundation of a secure life.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses like 6, 9, and 21 prescribe conduct directly, do not exalt yourself, do not betray confidences, feed your hungry enemy, while the many similes, verses 11 through 20 especially, mostly describe the effect of certain kinds of speech or character rather than issuing direct commands, inviting reflection rather than bare obedience.

debates.question: Does 'heap burning coals on his head,' verse 22, mean kindness to an enemy is really a means of securing their punishment or humiliation?

views.label: Coals of fire picture intensified judgment on the enemy
views.case: Some read the coals as an image of divine punishment being stored up, so that kindness becomes a subtle way of increasing the enemy's eventual condemnation.

views.label: Coals of fire picture shame that leads to repentance and change
views.case: The ancient Egyptian practice of carrying a pan of hot coals on the head as a public sign of repentance is often cited as background, suggesting the image pictures kindness producing shame that can soften an enemy's heart, consistent with Paul's use of the proverb in Romans 12:20-21 to urge overcoming evil with good, not securing an enemy's ruin.

debates.bearing: Read alongside Paul's own application in Romans 12, the second view fits the passage's larger aim, transforming an enemy through unexpected kindness rather than merely storing up judgment against him, though Scripture leaves final judgment entirely to God regardless of the image's precise background.

study.originalLanguages: The Hebrew behind 'fitly spoken,' verse 11, davar al ofanav, pictures a word moving smoothly on its own wheels, perfectly timed and placed, like apples of gold set in silver. Ruach, spirit or temper, verse 28, pictures the inner self as a walled city; a person who cannot govern this ruach lies exposed and defenseless, connecting self-control directly to the security of one's whole life.

typology.type: Feeding a hungry enemy and heaping coals of kindness, 25:21-22
typology.fulfillment: Christ's command to love enemies, and His own enemy love displayed at the cross, Romans 5:10, Romans 12:20.

typology.type: The king who searches out what God conceals, 25:2
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the greater king in whom all treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden, Colossians 2:3.

study.christ: Paul quotes verses 21-22 nearly word for word in Romans 12:20, commanding believers to feed a hungry enemy and give a thirsty one drink, showing that Christ's radical ethic of enemy love, and its final embodiment at the cross where He died for those still His enemies, Romans 5:10, was already anticipated in this ancient Hezekiah-era proverb. And the chapter's praise for the king's glory in searching out what God conceals, verse 2, is fulfilled in Christ, the true and greater Solomon, in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, Colossians 2:3, the mystery of God finally revealed.
study.god: It is God's own glory to conceal a matter and leave mystery for kings, and by extension all seekers of wisdom, to search out, verse 2, showing that not all truth is meant to be immediately obvious, and that patient searching itself honors Him. He also rewards kindness shown even to an enemy, verse 22, revealing a character more concerned with the possibility of repentance and restoration than with mere retribution.

crossReferences.note: Paul directly quotes Proverbs 25:21-22 on feeding a hungry enemy.
In Christ are hidden all treasures of wisdom, fulfilling the king's search in Proverbs 25:2.
Love your enemies, developing the enemy kindness commanded in Proverbs 25:21.
Whoever does not stumble in speech is perfect, echoing the self-control of Proverbs 25:28.

study.oneStory: This chapter honors the search for God's hidden wisdom, prizes well-timed and honest words, and commands surprising kindness even toward enemies. All three threads converge in Christ, the true king in whom wisdom's treasures are hidden and whose enemy love at the cross fulfills what this ancient Hezekiah-era proverb could only foreshadow.

quiz.q: According to verse 1, who preserved this collection of Solomon's proverbs?

options.text: The men of Hezekiah, king of Judah.
options.note: This chapter opens a new, explicitly credited collection copied generations after Solomon.

options.text: Solomon himself, writing them all at one time.
options.note: The proverbs originated with Solomon, but this collection specifically credits Hezekiah's scribes with copying them.

options.text: The prophet Isaiah.
options.note: Isaiah is not named as the source of this collection.

options.text: An unnamed group of anonymous scribes with no royal connection.
options.note: The scribes are specifically identified as working under King Hezekiah, not anonymous or unconnected to royal sponsorship.

quiz.q: What does verse 2 say is the glory of kings?

options.text: To search out a matter that it is God's glory to conceal.
options.note: Human rulers are given the dignity of investigation and discovery.

options.text: To conceal matters just as God does.
options.note: Concealing is specifically God's glory; searching out is the glory assigned to kings.

options.text: To rule with absolute, unquestioned power.
options.note: The verse's concern is searching out hidden matters, not raw power.

options.text: To avoid all difficult or mysterious questions.
options.note: This is nearly the opposite; the king's glory is precisely in pursuing what is concealed, not avoiding it.

quiz.q: What does verse 11 compare a word fitly spoken to?

options.text: Apples of gold in settings of silver.
options.note: Well-timed speech is prized as a rare and beautiful treasure.

options.text: A club or sharp arrow.
options.note: That image describes false testimony in verse 18, the opposite of a fitly spoken word.

options.text: Cold water to a weary soul.
options.note: That image describes good news from a distant land in verse 25, a related but distinct comparison.

options.text: Honey eaten in excess.
options.note: That image, from verse 27, warns against excess rather than praising fitly spoken words.

quiz.q: What does verse 21-22 instruct regarding a hungry or thirsty enemy?

options.text: Give him food and water, heaping burning coals on his head, and the LORD will reward you.
options.note: Kindness to an enemy is commended and directly rewarded by God.

options.text: Refuse to help him until he apologizes first.
options.note: This is the opposite of what the verses command.

options.text: Report him to the authorities instead of helping.
options.note: The verses' focus is personal kindness, not a call to report the enemy.

options.text: Help him only if there is no risk to yourself.
options.note: The verses give an unconditional command to help, without this qualification.

quiz.q: What does verse 28 compare a man who does not control his temper to?

options.text: A city whose walls are broken down.
options.note: Lack of self-control leaves a person utterly exposed and defenseless.

options.text: A well-fortified city.
options.note: This is the opposite; the image is of a city defenseless due to broken walls.

options.text: A quiet, undisturbed garden.
options.note: This image does not appear in the verse.

options.text: A city with strong walls but no gate.
options.note: The verse's image is total exposure, broken walls entirely, not merely a missing gate.

questions: Verse 2 says it is the glory of kings to search out what God conceals. Where in your own life is God inviting patient searching rather than demanding instant answers?
A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold, verse 11. Think of a recent conversation. Was your timing as wise as your words?
Verses 21-22 command kindness to a hungry or thirsty enemy. Who is someone that has wronged you whom you could practically bless this week?
Verse 17 warns against overstaying your welcome in a neighbor's house. Is there a relationship where you need to practice better boundaries, whether visiting too often or demanding too much?
Verse 28 pictures a person without self-control as a city with broken walls. Where is your own 'wall' down right now, in anger, appetite, or speech, leaving you exposed?

translationNotes.note: 'Copied by the men of Hezekiah' reflects a Hebrew verb for transcribing or compiling, evidence of an active scribal tradition preserving older wisdom material centuries after Solomon's reign.
'Fitly spoken' translates a rare Hebrew phrase, literally 'upon its wheels,' whose exact sense is debated but generally understood to mean rightly timed or aptly placed.

'Burning coals on his head' likely alludes to an ancient Egyptian penitential custom of carrying hot coals atop the head as a sign of shame and repentance, supporting a reading of transformative shame rather than vindictive punishment.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 25. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
